document: Discharging Preterm Infants Home on Caffeine, a Single Center Experience.
authors: Cheng Ma, Denisse Broadbent, Garrett Levin, Sanjeet Panda, Devaraj Sambalingam, Norma Garcia, Edson Ruiz and Ajay Pratap Singh
authors_snippet:
Cheng Ma 1, Denisse Broadbent 1,2, Garrett Levin 1, Sanjeet Panda 1,2, Devaraj Sambalingam 1,2,†,
Norma Garcia 1, Edson Ruiz 1,2 and Ajay Pratap Singh 1,2,*
year_of_publication_snippet: Received: 22 June 2020; Accepted: 24 August 2020; Published: 28 August 2020
country_snippet:
This study was reviewed by Institutional review board for the protection of human subjects at
Texas Tech University Health Sciences Center at El Paso, Texas. USA and approved with a waiver
of consent.
indication_snippet: Background: Apnea of prematurity (AOP) affects preterm neonates.
population: Preterm infants discharged home from the neonatal intensive care unit (NICU) on caffeine. Infants were born less than 30 weeks gestation, with some requiring oxygen therapy at discharge.
population_snippet:
After IRB approval, preterm infants discharged home from the neonatal intensive care unit (NICU)
on caffeine were compared with those without a discharge prescription for the period of January 2013
to December 2017.
intervention: Discharging preterm infants home on caffeine for Apnea of Prematurity (AOP)
intervention_snippet: Our objective was to assess safety and benefits, if any, of discharging preterm infants home on caffeine.
comparator: preterm infants without a discharge prescription for caffeine
comparator_snippet:
After IRB approval, preterm infants discharged home from the neonatal intensive care unit (NICU)
on caffeine were compared with those without a discharge prescription for the period of January 2013
to December 2017.
outcome: Length of stay, duration of caffeine use at home, cost of caffeine and apnea monitor use at home, emergency department (ED) visits for morbidities, hospitalizations, deaths.
outcome_snippet:
Primary outcomes of interest were length of stay, duration of caffeine usage at home, cost of
caffeine and apnea monitor use at home. Secondary outcomes included emergency department
(ED) visits for morbidities, including upper respiratory infections, brief resolved unexplained events
(BRUE) [15], other reasons and number of deaths during the first year of life.
safety_snippet: Our objective was to assess safety and benefits, if any, of discharging preterm infants home on caffeine.
study_design: Observational study with economic component (retrospective cohort study)
study_design_snippet: Infants discharged home on caffeine compared to infants not discharged on caffeine were more likely to have been diagnosed with bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD) [33% vs. 22%, p value = <0.05] as defined per National Institute of Child Health and Human Development (NICHD) consensus statement [16,17], and require oxygen therapy at discharge [24% vs. 13%, p value = <0.05].
perspective_snippet: The majority of the patients were on Medicaid, and the average cost of an apnea monitor and caffeine at home was USD 1326 ± 970 and USD 50 ± 34 per 30 days, based on Medicaid reimbursements of Apnea monitor use per 30 days and 10 bottles of generic caffeine citrate (60 mg/3 mL oral solution) for a 30 day supply.
time_horizon_snippet: Data was collected from electronic medical records (EMR) from the time of admission through the 1st year of life.
discount_rate_snippet: Abstract: Background: Apnea of prematurity (AOP) affects preterm neonates.
currency_snippet: The average cost of an apnea monitor and caffeine at home was USD 1326 ± 970 and USD 50 ± 34 per 30 days, based on Medicaid reimbursements
currency_year_snippet:
After IRB approval, preterm infants discharged home from the neonatal intensive care unit (NICU)
on caffeine were compared with those without a discharge prescription for the period of January 2013
to December 2017.
total_cost_per_patient_snippet: The average cost of an apnea monitor and caffeine at home per 30 days was USD 1326 ± 970 and USD 50 ± 34 per 30 days, based on Medicaid reimbursements
comparator_cost_snippet: The average cost of an apnea monitor and caffeine at home was USD 1326 ± 970 and USD 50 ± 34 per 30 days, based on Medicaid reimbursements
incremental_cost_snippet: The average cost of an apnea monitor and caffeine at home per 30 days was USD 1326 and USD 50.
outcome_measure: Cost per episode avoided
outcome_measure_snippet: This study shows discharging preterm infant might also be cost effective. We show that average cost of use of apnea monitor was USD 1326 and caffeine medication at home was USD 50 per 30 days use for each individual patient. These infants were on caffeine after discharge for a median of 31 days. If they were to stay in NICU until complete resolution of AOP, that may have resulted in an increasing total cost of hospitalization and, based on previous studies [19-21], this likely resulted in average estimated savings of USD 77,500 per patient, basing on a conservative estimate of USD 2500 per day NICU charges.
icer_snippet: Abstract: Background: Apnea of prematurity (AOP) affects preterm neonates.
threshold_referenced_snippet: Abstract: Background: Apnea of prematurity (AOP) affects preterm neonates.
conclusion: cost-effective
conclusion_snippet: This study shows discharging preterm infant might also be cost effective.
quality_snippet: There are several limitations to this study. First, this is a retrospective study, and several unknown factors may influence the outcomes.

document: A cost-effectiveness analysis for high versus standard (low) dose caffeine for the treatment of apnea in neonatal intensive care unit.
authors: Eilan Al-Hersh, Dina Abushanab, Fouad AbouNahia, Daniel Rainkie, Moza Al Hail, Palli Valapila Abdulrouf, Wessam El-Kassem & Daoud Al-Badriyeh
authors_snippet: Eilan Al-Hersh, Dina Abushanab, Fouad AbouNahia, Daniel Rainkie, Moza Al Hail, Palli Valapila Abdulrouf, Wessam El-Kassem & Daoud Al-Badriyeh
source_type_snippet: Journal of Pharmaceutical Policy and Practice
country_snippet: Methods: From the hospital perspective of Hamad Medical Corporation (HMC), Qatar, a cost-effectiveness decision-analytic model was constructed to follow the use of a HD maintenance caffeine of 20 mg/kg/dose versus a LD maintenance caffeine of 10 mg/kg/dose, in a simulated cohort of AOP neonates, over a therapy follow-up duration of six weeks, until neonatal intensive care (NICU) discharge. The clinical inputs were primarily literature-based, while the resource cost and utilisation were locally extracted in HMC.
indication_snippet: Objective: Preterm babies are prone to experiencing apnea of prematurity (AOP), mostly characterised by a pause in breathing lasting a minimum of 20 seconds. Recent literature supported higher maintenance doses of caffeine, indicating benefits. This study evaluated the cost-effectiveness of high maintenance dose (HD) versus low maintenance dose (LD) caffeine for AOP in neonates.
population: Preterm infants with apnea of prematurity (AOP) in neonatal intensive care unit (NICU)
population_snippet: Objective: Preterm babies are prone to experiencing apnea of prematurity (AOP), mostly characterised by a pause in breathing lasting a minimum of 20 seconds. Recent literature supported higher maintenance doses of caffeine, indicating benefits. This study evaluated the cost-effectiveness of high maintenance dose (HD) versus low maintenance dose (LD) caffeine for AOP in neonates.
intervention: high maintenance dose (HD) caffeine of 20 mg/kg/dose
intervention_snippet:
This study evaluated the cost-effectiveness of high maintenance dose (HD) versus low maintenance dose (LD) caffeine for AOP in neonates.
Methods: From the hospital perspective of Hamad Medical Corporation (HMC), Qatar, a cost-effectiveness decision-analytic model was constructed to follow the use of a HD maintenance caffeine of 20 mg/kg/dose versus a LD maintenance caffeine of 10 mg/kg/dose, in a simulated cohort of AOP neonates, over a therapy follow-up duration of six weeks, until neonatal intensive care (NICU) discharge.
comparator: low maintenance dose of caffeine, defined as 10 mg/kg
comparator_snippet: The two dose regimens of caffeine that are compared in the current economic model were the low maintenance dose of caffeine, defined as 10 mg/ kg, and the high maintenance dose of the caffeine, defined as 20 mg/kg. Both were intravenously administered.
outcome: ICER, incremental cost, length of stay
outcome_snippet: The cost-effectiveness outcome measure was calculated per therapy success, defined as survival with no apnea and successful extubation removal within 72 hours, with or without adverse events.
safety: intraventricular haemorrhage (IVH), periventricular leukomalacia (PVL), necrotising enterocolitis (NEC), retinopathy of prematurity (ROP), patent ductus arteriosus (PDA), bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD), and sepsis, tachycardia
safety_snippet:
The relevant AEs that may take place with success are intraventricular haem-orrbage (IVH), periventricular leukomalacia (PVL), necrotising enterocolitis (NEC), retinopathy of prematurity (ROP), patent ductus arteriosus (PDA), bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD), and sepsis. Only grades 3 and 4 for each of IVH and ROP were considered, while grades 2 and 3 were considered for NEC. The remaining grades were excluded due to the assumption that these grades are mild and do not substantially affect the cost management of the infant.
In contrast, failure was defined as either (i) discontinuation of caffeine due to severe AEs, (ii) death, or (iii) extubation failure due to the persistence of apnea. Premature discontinuation is an incomplete course of caffeine due to adverse drug reactions (ADRs). The ADR of interest in the model is tachy-cardia, defined as a heart rate greater than 160–180 bpm (Ban, 2017).
study_design_snippet: Methods: From the hospital perspective of Hamad Medical Corporation (HMC), Qatar, a cost-effectiveness decision-analytic model was constructed to follow the use of a HD maintenance caffeine of 20 mg/kg/dose versus a LD maintenance caffeine of 10 mg/kg/dose, in a simulated cohort of AOP neonates, over a therapy follow-up duration of six weeks, until neonatal intensive care (NICU) discharge.
perspective_snippet: As the model was developed from the public hospital perspective (i.e. HMC, the primary health care provider in Qatar).
time_horizon_snippet:
The model follow-up duration was until 37 weeks post-menstrual age (PMA)
As per HMC practice, infants receive therapy until successful AOP mitigation by 37 weeks PMA.
discount_rate_snippet: Given the acute nature of the condition and the therapy, no discounting of costs was undertaken.
currency_snippet: The cost was reported in United States dollar (US$), based on 2023 exchange rates (1 Qatari Riyal [QAR] = 0.27 US$) and inflated to 2023 using the Consumer Price Index for Medical Care, when needed (Qatar Inflation Rate, 2023).
currency_year_snippet: The cost was reported in United States dollar (US$), based on 2023 exchange rates (1 Qatari Riyal [QAR] = 0.27 US$) and inflated to 2023 using the Consumer Price Index for Medical Care, when needed (Qatar Inflation Rate, 2023).
total_cost_per_patient_snippet: Overall, the high dose caffeine has a higher cost of US$ 100,389 (95% CI, US$ 100,145–100,632), compared to low dose caffeine (US$ 96,520) (95%CI,
comparator_cost_snippet: Overall, the high dose caffeine has a higher cost of US$ 100,389 (95% CI, US$ 100,145–100,632), compared to low dose caffeine (US$ 96,520) (95%CI,
incremental_cost_snippet: Therefore, the mean difference in cost is US$ 3869 (95% CI, US$ 3823–3915) with the low dose over high dose caffeine.
outcome_measure: additional case of success
outcome_measure_snippet: The cost-effectiveness outcome measure was calculated per therapy success, defined as survival with no apnea and successful extubation removal within 72 hours, with or without adverse events.
icer: US$ 16,895 (95% CI, US$ 15,242–18,549) per additional case of success
icer_snippet: The mean ICER was calculated to be US$ 16,895 (95% CI, US$ 15,242–18,549) with high dose over low dose caffeine per additional case of success.
threshold_referenced_snippet: cost-effectiveness threshold is estimated to be USD 150,000 per case of success, in line with relevant Qatari studies (Abushanab et al., 2022; Kaddoura et al., 2022; Al-Badriyeh et al., 2022).
conclusion: cost-effective
conclusion_snippet: Conclusion: This is the first literature economic evaluation of caffeine for AOP. Despite increasing the cost of therapy, HD maintenance caffeine seems to be a cost-effective alternative to LD caffeine in Qatar. Our results support the recent global trends of increased use of HD caffeine for AOP in NICU.
quality_snippet: A cost-effectiveness analysis for high versus standard (low) dose caffeine for the treatment of apnea in neonatal intensive care unit

document: The Use of Caffeine Citrate in the Management of Neonatal Apnea in Low- and Middle-Income Countries: A Rapid Systematic Review.
authors: Seth Kwabena Amponsah, Chris Mensah Nartey, Emmanuel Kwaku Ofori
authors_snippet: Seth Kwabena Amponsah¹ | Chris Mensah Nartey¹ | Emmanuel Kwaku Ofori²
country_snippet:
TABLE 2 | Number of neonates with recurrent apnea associated with the use of caffeine citrate versus aminophylline in the management of neonatal apnea.
Authors/year	Country
Zulqarnain et al. [40]	Pakistan
indication_snippet: The Use of Caffeine Citrate in the Management of Neonatal Apnea in Low- and Middle-Income Countries: A Rapid Systematic Review.
population: Neonates with apnea of prematurity (AOP) in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs)
population_snippet: Background and Aims: Caffeine citrate is an example of a methylxanthine approved for managing apnea of prematurity (AOP). However, there is limited evidence of its use in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs). This rapid systematic review aims to appraise the literature on using caffeine citrate in managing neonatal apnea in LMICs.
intervention_snippet: The Use of Caffeine Citrate in the Management of Neonatal Apnea in Low- and Middle-Income Countries: A Rapid Systematic Review.
comparator_snippet: Methods: A comprehensive search was conducted on literature reporting the treatment of AOP in LMICs. The search was based on a population, intervention, comparison, and outcome format using medical subject heading terms. The PRISMA and PRISMA extension for scoping reviews guidelines were meticulously followed. PubMed, Science Direct, and Scopus were among the bibliographic databases searched.
outcome: Efficacy, safety, and cost-effectiveness of caffeine citrate in the management of neonatal apnea, including recurrent apnea, adverse effects (tachycardia, gastrointestinal, central nervous system, hyperglycemia), and cost per vial.
outcome_snippet: Results: The evaluation of 10 studies involving 1010 preterm infants provided compelling evidence. Our findings demonstrated that caffeine citrate, compared to aminophylline, had fewer adverse effects. The adverse effects, including feeding intolerance, tachycardia, central nervous system derailment, and hyperglycemia, were significantly reduced with caffeine citrate. Furthermore, data from the included studies revealed that caffeine citrate had a lower risk of recurrent apnea and was less likely to fall out of the recommended therapeutic range than aminophylline. These results unequivocally establish caffeine citrate's safety, efficacy, and cost-effectiveness in treating prematurity apnea in LMICS, providing a reliable and beneficial intervention for neonatal care in these regions.
safety_snippet: The meta-analysis from the interventional studies revealed no significant risk of hyperglycemia in the caffeine citrate group compared to aminophylline (RR= 0.59, 95% CI = -0.493 to 01.68, p = 0.698).
study_design_snippet: The Use of Caffeine Citrate in the Management of Neonatal Apnea in Low- and Middle-Income Countries: A Rapid Systematic Review.
perspective_snippet: This rapid systematic review aimed to provide evidence of caffeine citrate's efficacy, safety, and cost-effectiveness in managing AOP in LMICS.
time_horizon_snippet: This rapid systematic review sought to assess caffeine citrate's efficacy, safety, and/or cost-effectiveness in LMICs. The review focused on studies published between 2000 and 2023.
discount_rate_snippet: This rapid systematic review aims to appraise the literature on using caffeine citrate in managing neonatal apnea in LMICs.
currency_snippet: In Ghana, Kenya, Nigeria, Tanzania, and Uganda, the price of caffeine citrate per 3 mL vial ranges from $1.73 to 73.63 (Figure 6).
currency_year_snippet: In Ghana, Kenya, Nigeria, Tanzania, and Uganda, the price of caffeine citrate per 3 mL vial ranges from $1.73 to 73.63 (Figure 6).
total_cost_per_patient_snippet: The average end-customer price for a 25 mg/mL vial of caffeine citrate was $2.7 in India [41].
comparator_cost_snippet: In Ghana, Kenya, Nigeria, Tanzania, and Uganda, the price of caffeine citrate per 3 mL vial ranges from $1.73 to 73.63 (Figure 6).
incremental_cost_snippet:
3.3 | Cost-Effectiveness of Caffeine Citrate
In Ghana, Kenya, Nigeria, Tanzania, and Uganda, the price of caffeine citrate per 3 mL vial ranges from $1.73 to 73.63 (Figure 6).
outcome_measure: Relative risk (RR) of recurrent apnea, tachycardia, gastrointestinal adverse effects, central nervous system adverse effects, hyperglycemia; median pulse rate; serum concentrations; drug price; length of hospital stay.
outcome_measure_snippet: Our findings demonstrated that caffeine citrate, compared to aminophylline, had fewer adverse effects. The adverse effects, including feeding intolerance, tachycardia, central nervous system derailment, and hyperglycemia, were significantly reduced with caffeine citrate. Furthermore, data from the included studies revealed that caffeine citrate had a lower risk of recurrent apnea and was less likely to fall out of the recommended therapeutic range than aminophylline. These results unequivocally establish caffeine citrate's safety, efficacy, and cost-effectiveness in treating prematurity apnea in LMICS, providing a reliable and beneficial intervention for neonatal care in these regions.
icer_snippet: Results: The evaluation of 10 studies involving 1010 preterm infants provided compelling evidence. ... These results unequivocally establish caffeine citrate's safety, efficacy, and cost-effectiveness in treating prematurity apnea in LMICS...
threshold_referenced_snippet: Our findings demonstrated that caffeine citrate, compared to aminophylline, had fewer adverse effects. ... These results unequivocally establish caffeine citrate's safety, efficacy, and cost-effectiveness in treating prematurity apnea in LMICS...
conclusion: Caffeine may be a preferred option in managing AOP in LMICs. However, high drug prices and lack of availability of caffeine may be factors limiting its use in these settings. Overall, caffeine citrate's safety, efficacy, and cost-effectiveness in treating prematurity apnea in LMICs are unequivocally established.
conclusion_snippet: Conclusion: Caffeine may be a preferred option in managing AOP in LMICs. However, high drug prices and lack of availability of caffeine may be factors limiting its use in these settings.
quality_snippet: The Newcastle-Ottawa Scale was used to assess the risk of bias in cohort studies based on patient selection, comparability, and outcome assessment. Scores ranging from 7 to 9 represented high-quality articles, 5-7 represented moderate-quality studies, and 0-5 represented low-quality studies (Table S1). Sensitivity analysis was employed to determine the impact of a single study on the combined effect size of a single study on pooled estimates (Table S3). The Critical Appraisal Skills Program for Randomized Control Trials checklist was also used to evaluate the quality of Randomized Control Trials.

discount_rate_snippet: This rapid systematic review aims to appraise the literature on using caffeine citrate in managing neonatal apnea in LMICs.
currency_snippet: In Ghana, Kenya, Nigeria, Tanzania, and Uganda, the price of caffeine citrate per 3 mL vial ranges from $1.73 to 73.63 (Figure 6).
currency_year_snippet: In Ghana, Kenya, Nigeria, Tanzania, and Uganda, the price of caffeine citrate per 3 mL vial ranges from $1.73 to 73.63 (Figure 6).
total_cost_per_patient: $2.7 for a 25 mg/mL vial of caffeine citrate
total_cost_per_patient_snippet: The average end-customer price for a 25 mg/mL vial of caffeine citrate was $2.7 in India [41].
comparator_cost_snippet: In Ghana, Kenya, Nigeria, Tanzania, and Uganda, the price of caffeine citrate per 3 mL vial ranges from $1.73 to 73.63 (Figure 6).
incremental_cost_snippet:
3.3 | Cost-Effectiveness of Caffeine Citrate
In Ghana, Kenya, Nigeria, Tanzania, and Uganda, the price of caffeine citrate per 3 mL vial ranges from $1.73 to 73.63 (Figure 6).
outcome_measure: Relative risk (RR) of recurrent apnea, tachycardia, gastrointestinal adverse effects, central nervous system adverse effects, hyperglycemia; median pulse rate; serum concentrations; drug price; length of hospital stay.
outcome_measure_snippet: Our findings demonstrated that caffeine citrate, compared to aminophylline, had fewer adverse effects. The adverse effects, including feeding intolerance, tachycardia, central nervous system derailment, and hyperglycemia, were significantly reduced with caffeine citrate. Furthermore, data from the included studies revealed that caffeine citrate had a lower risk of recurrent apnea and was less likely to fall out of the recommended therapeutic range than aminophylline. These results unequivocally establish caffeine citrate's safety, efficacy, and cost-effectiveness in treating prematurity apnea in LMICS, providing a reliable and beneficial intervention for neonatal care in these regions.
icer_snippet: Results: The evaluation of 10 studies involving 1010 preterm infants provided compelling evidence. ... These results unequivocally establish caffeine citrate's safety, efficacy, and cost-effectiveness in treating prematurity apnea in LMICS...
threshold_referenced_snippet: Our findings demonstrated that caffeine citrate, compared to aminophylline, had fewer adverse effects. ... These results unequivocally establish caffeine citrate's safety, efficacy, and cost-effectiveness in treating prematurity apnea in LMICS...
conclusion: Caffeine may be a preferred option in managing AOP in LMICs. However, high drug prices and lack of availability of caffeine may be factors limiting its use in these settings. Overall, caffeine citrate's safety, efficacy, and cost-effectiveness in treating prematurity apnea in LMICs are unequivocally established.
conclusion_snippet: Conclusion: Caffeine may be a preferred option in managing AOP in LMICs. However, high drug prices and lack of availability of caffeine may be factors limiting its use in these settings.
quality_snippet: The Newcastle-Ottawa Scale was used to assess the risk of bias in cohort studies based on patient selection, comparability, and outcome assessment. Scores ranging from 7 to 9 represented high-quality articles, 5-7 represented moderate-quality studies, and 0-5 represented low-quality studies (Table S1). Sensitivity analysis was employed to determine the impact of a single study on the combined effect size of a single study on pooled estimates (Table S3). The Critical Appraisal Skills Program for Randomized Control Trials checklist was also used to evaluate the quality of Randomized Control Trials.

threshold_referenced_snippet: Is caffeine available and affordable in low and middle-income countries? A survey in sub-Saharan Africa
conclusion: Caffeine availability and affordability vary between and within sub-Saharan African countries. High price and lack of availability are significant barriers, along with complex government regulations and lack of demand (due to beliefs other methylxanthines are suitable substitutes and knowledge gaps).
conclusion_snippet: Caffeine availability and affordability vary between and within sub-Saharan African countries. The high price of caffeine in reference to the poverty rates in these low- and middle-income countries appears to be the most significant barrier to caffeine availability. Other factors limiting availability may be the presence of complex and challenging government regulations, and a lack of demand by practitioners founded on the belief that other methylxanthines are suitable substitutes for caffeine. Such beliefs, and poor knowledge that caffeine is on the WHO essential drug list, point to a sizeable knowledge gap that must be bridged.
